headings.text: The Quiet Return of the Iberian Lynx
The Long Drought of the American West

paragraphs.text: Forty Issues · Five Years · One Field of View

When we shipped the first folded sheet of Chlorophyll Dispatch from a leaking pressroom in Galway in the spring of 2020, we did not yet know whether long-form environmental journalism still had a constituency. Forty issues later, the archive you are reading below is the answer: a slow, deliberate ledger of bog water and river chemistry, of land disputes settled by patience and unsettled by drought, of correspondents who learned to file copy from places without addresses. We have re-indexed each issue here for the working researcher and the curious reader alike — every cover story, every featured dispatch, every dateline. Eight of these entries are marked as Editor's Selection: pieces we still send to new staff on their first day. The rest are sequenced strictly by month of publication. Read backwards into our weather; read forwards into our argument. Either way, the chlorophyll keeps moving.
— M. Halloran, Editor-in-Chief · Connemara, December 2024
A three-part field investigation into the political economy of Irish peat extraction, the science of Sphagnum recovery, and the women rewetting the western moors.
How four decades of fertilizer subsidies remade the dissolved-oxygen profile of the Mississippi watershed — and what eight estuarine scientists now propose.
A reported essay on Indigenous ice-core science, language preservation, and the bilingual training program that has placed twenty-six glaciologists on the Cordillera.
Inside the global underground fungal sampling initiative, where ten thousand soil cores are redrawing our maps of forest cooperation and carbon flux.

A six-month embed with reintroduction biologists in the Sierra Morena, tracing the species' improbable climb from twenty-five individuals in 2002 to a working population of more than two thousand — and the rabbit-disease economy that nearly broke it.
Salinity intrusion in the Bengal delta, the collapse of three fisher cooperatives, and the cyclone-shelter architecture being reverse-engineered from village memory.
Ten estates, three rewilding philosophies, and an ongoing argument about whether the Cairngorms should be tended as a museum or released as a system.
On the dyke that resurrected the northern lobe, the carp fishery now feeding three towns, and the unresolved question of who owns a sea that comes back.
The unlicensed reintroductions that quietly remade a Perthshire catchment, and the legal recognition that arrived a decade after the dams were already holding water.
The satellite calibration project tracking how mineral plumes from the Bodélé Depression keep a rainforest fertile, and what its forecast decline could mean.
A profile of the environmental-law clinic that has filed seventeen cases on behalf of the piping plover and rewritten three states' beach-management codes.
A long walk with the last of the criollo herders as their leases expire and a million hectares are folded into a new conservation corridor — reported across four seasons.
North-facing slopes, deep ravines, cold-air pools: a tour of the small geographies that buffer species against warming, and the conservation tactic that follows.
Hydrology, royalties, and flamingo counts on the salt flats: a six-part audit of the trade-offs underwriting the electric-vehicle transition.
A reported feature on the slow-steaming protocols, propeller redesigns, and acoustic monitoring networks now buying back hearing-room for cetaceans.
An inventory year inside the global crop diversity backup — and the seven national seed banks that have already withdrawn against it.
Reporting from three fire camps in Sakha and Yukon, on a season that turned the boreal from carbon sink to net emitter for the third consecutive year.
How a continental citizen-science census added forty-one species to the European bee record and quietly removed nineteen others.
Two years inside Latin America's first constitutional rights-of-nature tribunal: thirty-eight cases, four landmark rulings, and the still-unresolved question of how to compensate a river.
A two-decade seed-broadcasting experiment that has resurrected nine thousand acres of meadow, and the bay-scallop fishery now riding its recovery.
Three hundred small-hydro permits, eleven NGOs, and the slow legal counterattack defending the Vjosa and her sister rivers from cascade development.
An illustrated essay on what a single shovel-cut reveals about agricultural history, and why the under-mapped subsoil is the next climate frontier.
Fifteen years after the pathogen swept Monteverde, an account of the species that returned, the ones that did not, and the resistance now being inherited.
Inside the third-party verifiers grading offset projects, the methodologies they apply, and the credit classes already withdrawn from the registry.
A reported account of the genomic surveillance project tracking Schistocerca outbreaks, and the trans-border pesticide accord that nearly didn't pass.
Twenty-two years of declining flow on the Colorado, the seven-state allocation argument it has triggered, and the tribal water rights now decisively on the table — a six-month embed across the basin.
The first contained-release of a self-limiting Aedes line on a Caribbean island, and the regulatory architecture being built behind it.
A reporting trip on the ROV vessels mapping Lophelia pertusa reefs, and the trawling-ban geometry that has slowed but not stopped their fragmentation.
How a single species reconfigured grazing leases, oil-and-gas setbacks, and county politics across the sagebrush sea — and what the listing decision did next.
Reporting on the lakebed cores that may anchor the Anthropocene, and the international stratigraphy commission still arguing about the spike.
Inside the Apulian quarantine zones, the cultivar-resistance trials, and the rural depopulation already underway in groves felled by bacterial infection.
The first national inventories crediting coastal wetland sequestration, and the property-line disputes the accounting has surfaced.
Seismologists, ornithologists, and air-quality scientists on the natural experiment of a stalled spring, and what each discipline learned that it could not unlearn.
Reporting from New South Wales on epicormic recovery, the species that did not return, and the koala census redrawn by smoke.
Eight nations, one herd: the trans-boundary buffalo agreement and the first cross-Medicine-Line release in more than a century.

The lidar campaign that revised central African forest carbon estimates upward by eleven percent, and the ground-truth crews — paid in francs and contested permits — who made the satellite numbers stand.
Aerial surveys, in-water transects, and the politics of declaring an event over: a reported chronology of Australia's most-watched coral system.
A long winter with the trapping teams documenting Gulo gulo's contracting denning range across the northern Rockies and the listing decision still pending.
How wind-trajectory modeling redrew the early-warning maps for the Horn of Africa, and the under-funded agencies trying to use them.
The inaugural Connemara dispatch on bog drainage, republished here with the original notes and four follow-up reports filed across the subsequent decade.
A slower wayto read the field.
Eight issues a year, printed on uncoated stock, posted in unbleached envelopes. Each subscription supports a working field correspondent for one full reporting cycle.
Chlorophyll Dispatch — long-form reporting from the green edge of the record.
Founded 2020 · Galway & Edinburgh · Printed quarterly on uncoated stock, mailed worldwide.
